title: The Macro Minute: Will central banks look through the global energy supply shock?

your skipper here at Darius Dale to
present our macro minute for Thursday,
March 19th, 2026. Hope everyone's having
a great week. So, I'm joining you guys
live from Houston down here in Texas and
just wanted to give a shout out to my
favorite MAC 7 company. I was invited to
present at a private
corporate executives event 405 hundred
executives event down here
and obviously it's confidential so I
can't share any details, but the one
thing I will say
is the world is not at all prepared
for how powerful AI is.
I think we're going to be very right,
almost too right
on our jobless recovery thesis, not just
with respect to the US economy, but with
respect to the global economy.
The capabilities that I witnessed, I was
privy privileged to witness yesterday
for AI are well beyond what the median
investor
and the median executive realizes is
capable of today. I'm not talking about
tomorrow's AI, today's AI is
significantly more advanced than I think
the average, the median investor and the
median corporate executive realizes. So,
this is this is going to be big for
society.
I guarantee it's going to be a lot
bigger for society than investors are
realizing and I'm not entirely sure that
it's not going to be really big for the
markets in a negative way in terms of
the impact on the labor markets around
the world, but we'll see. You know,
that's a bridge we got to cross when we
get there.
For now, today's bridge is our executive
summary from today's lead off morning
notes, so let's just dive right in. And
before I dive right in, I will also say
this is probably the most information
I've ever analyzed in one morning in my
near two-decade career on global Wall
Street. So, if you're you like to nerd
it out and you want to get a real deep
dive into how, you know, the best
institutional investors around the world
think about, you know, measuring and
mapping the evolving distribution of
probable economic policy and market
outcomes, then boy oh boy, you're going
to enjoy today's macro minute.
If you're not a nerd and you don't like
that stuff, then boy oh boy, you're
going to love kissing Dr. Mo cuz they
simplify everything in actionable uh
signals uh you know, for even um you
know, what we're going to talk about
later for you and uh eighth graders to
understand. So, without further delay,
let's just get into it. Today's key
macro question is, will central banks
look through the global energy supply
shock?
Our answer is yes for now,
but marginally hawkish forward guidance
from the Fed, Bank of Japan, Bank of
England, and the European Central Bank
signal investors who have yet to lower
their gross and tighten their net
exposures will not be bailed out by
central banks should the global energy
supply shock devolve continue to
devolve.
Here are the most important things we
learned today regarding the six key
macro cycles that influence the momentum
and dispersion within and across asset
markets on a trend basis, starting with
the growth cycle.
Median FOMC member positively revised
their 2026, 2027, 2028, and longer-run
real GDP estimate.
The median FOMC member positively
revised their 2027 unemployment rate
forecast, which signals a boost in
productivity growth in the context of
the positive revision to real GDP in the
same year.
Looking to economic data, the strong
positive impulse in factory orders to
well above trend three-month annualized
rate in January supported our paradigm C
theme. Initial and continuing jobless
claims currently signal a low
probability of a developing recession in
the US economy. The 42 macro business
cycle model currently signals a low
probability of a developing recession in
the US economy as well. Elsewhere,
double-digit percentage declines in
total headcount from two internationally
relevant companies supported our jobless
recovery thesis.
HSBC Holdings is contemplating
substantial workforce reductions across
a multi-year time horizon as chief
executive officer Georges Elhedery uh
accelerates an AI-driven transformation
aimed at compressing middle and back
office functions.
Roles without direct client engagement
within global service hubs are expected
to bear the brunt of restructuring, with
approximately 20,000 positions, or
roughly 10% of total employees, at risk
of elimination. The contemplated
downsizing initiative is projected to
deliver a 5 to 6% reduction in
annualized group expenses and it's being
developed as part of a 3 to 5-year
strategic roadmap. Crypto.com announced
a workforce reduction of roughly 12% as
the digital asset platform recalibrates
its operating model to align with
accelerating AI capabilities and crypto
market conditions.
The Singapore-based exchange stated on
Thursday that broad AI integration
across its operations enables lower
staffing requirements translating to
about 8100 and 80 employees. Per chief
executive officer Chris Marzalek,
"Companies that do not make this pivot
immediately will fail.
Companies that move immediately and pair
the best AI tools with top performers
will achieve a level of scale and
precision that was previously thought to
be impossible.
This is where we must go."
Looking abroad, Japan's industrial
production and machine orders data,
January industrial production and
machine orders data did not support our
outlook for firmer growth, but the
positive revision to the BOJ's growth
forecast did.
The UK's January jobs report did not
support our outlook for moderating
growth.
Transition to the inflation cycle, the
median FOMC member positively revised
their 2026 and 2027 headline PCE
deflator forecast. The median FOMC
member positively revised their 2026 and
2027 core PCE deflator forecast as well.
Sticky inflation is
complicating the near-term policy
outlook amid a multi-year trend of
widening dispersion among estimates of
the neutral rate by FOMC members.
Notably, neither the maximum nor minimum
estimate of the neutral rate changed
despite the March 2026 summary of
economic projections incrementally
supporting our structural uptrend in
productivity growth thesis and sticky
inflation theme.
It is worth noting that long-term
inflation expectations are well anchored
relative to the 2021, 2022 inflation
episode. The 5-year 5-year inflation
swap rate has declined 13 basis points
since peaking in late January, which is
evidence of the hawkish impact that the
nomination of Kevin Warsh has had on
financial markets. Elsewhere, the strong
positive impulses in headline PPI, core
PPI, and services PPI, and the weak
positive impulse in core PPE ex-trade
services to respective well above trend
three-month SARs in February supported
our stake-in-inflation theme. Worse,
these elevated levels predate the energy
supply shock in March. Looking abroad,
the UK's January jobs report supported
our outlook for slowing inflation.
Shifting to the monetary policy cycle,
yes, this is a two-page executive
summary. Apologies for that. But again,
the data doesn't sleep. And the markets
certainly don't sleep, so you can sleep
on either the data and the markets, but
I guarantee your portfolio won't be
sleeping. It's going to be having
nightmares if you do. Or unless you pay
someone to do this for you. Uh in terms
of monetary policy cycle,
the positive revisions to real GDP,
unemployment rate, uh headline PCE, and
core PC deflator forecasts of the Fed's
March 2026 summary of economic
projections were incrementally
supportive of our paradigm C theme, our
structural uptrend in productivity
growth thesis, our jobless recovery
thesis, and our stake-in-inflation
theme. Uh for uh real quick, for those
of you who may not be unfamiliar with
what paradigm C means, paradigm C is the
grow phase of the cut-grow-print
sequence required to address the
geopolitically driven supply-demand
imbalance in the Treasury bond market,
uh which has been our core research view
since the summer of 2023.
I've officially lost count on how many
times I've front-run the Fed to
developing an accurate understanding of
economic dynamics and policy responses
throughout my career. Uh note, one
person outperforming the politicized
FOMC is not good for America. Getting
back into the data, the median FOMC
member left their 2026, 2027, 2028
policy rate forecast unchanged and
increased their estimate of the neutral
rate, which signals the politicized
Powell Fed uh generally views the
positive productivity shock as
increasing R-star, which is the exact
opposite view posited by Fed chair uh
nominee Kevin Warsh and said Governor
Stephen Moore.
Relative to history, Jerome Powell
remaining on the FOMC beyond the
expiration of his term as Fed chair is
indeed a politicized decision, albeit
one designed to counter a
hyper-politicized attack on the
institution's perceived independence,
which we've long argued will be eroded
in this fourth turning. Reminder, Powell
Fed's historic monetary policy errors
were and are a key contributor to the
K-shaped economy crisis and the
nationwide affordability crisis
underpinning it. Reminder, the federal
government accumulated nearly $6
trillion in deficits during the 2020 and
2021 during 2020 and 2021 and the Powell
Fed monetized nearly 6 for 60% of that
$6 trillion of deficit accumulation.
Reminder, the Powell Fed was performing
quantitative easing with reported CPI
greater than 8% in April of 2022 and
reported GDP of greater than 4% in the
first quarter of 2022, data in hand.
8% inflation, 4% real GDP growth, data
in hand.
The Fed refrained from making even minor
adjustments to its policy rate corridor
scheme,
signaling FOMC members are comfortable
with the new marginally hawkish
liquidity regime in the $13 trillion
repo market. Slowing down the pace of
reserve management purchases will be on
the table if the energy supply shock
persists.
The Fed's current restricted policy
setting is one rate cut away from
neutral according to our OIS market
implied estimate. All told, as predicted
in late 2021, short-term and long-term
interest rates in the US are making
higher highs and higher lows on a
secular basis.
Looking abroad, in the Eurozone, the
ECB's decision to leave its policy rates
unchanged is consistent with our outlook
for stable at target inflation. The Q4
labor cost data supported our outlook as
well. In Japan, the BOJ's decision to
leave policy rates unchanged and the
positive revision to its inflation
forecast is consistent with our outlook
for accelerating inflation. The Bank of
Japan positively revised its headline
CPI forecast for the next fiscal year.
The Bank of Japan positively revised its
core CPI forecast for the current, next,
and subsequent fiscal year, and the Bank
of Japan positively revised its real GDP
growth forecast for the current and next
fiscal year, and but negatively revised
its real GDP growth forecast for the
subsequent fiscal year. In the UK, the
Bank of England's decision to leave its
policy rates unchanged is consistent
with our outlook for persistent
above-target inflation.
Transitioning to the fiscal policy
cycle, the January Treasury
International Capital Tick data
supported our geopolitically driven
supply-demand imbalance in the Treasury
bond market thesis and signaled a
widening divergence between foreign
demand for Treasury coupon, which peaked
in November 2025, and foreign demand for
US corporate stocks and bonds, which is
at all-time highs. Nothing actionable
with respect to our Bayesian priors in
the liquidity and positioning cycles.
Continue reviewing to learn whether our
institutional grade quantitative risk
management overlays, KISS and Dr. Mo,
are evolving their signals in response
to these or anticipated fundamental
dynamics, which by the way, for those
maybe unfamiliar or new to 42 Macro
Research, we don't use any of this
top-flight macroeconomic forecasting and
and nowcasting insight to help our
clients and myself, because I use KISS
to manage my entire liquid net worth, uh
to you manage risk in global financial
markets. All of the pivots that we make
from an asset allocation and portfolio
construction perspective run through the
lens of our systematic quantitative KISS
and Dr. Mo strategies, which are much
better at timing financial market risk
in terms of increasing and reducing our
increasing reducing exposure across the
various asset classes and factors, uh
much better uh than I would ever be
trying to tie, you know, piece together
this massive puzzle, which continues to
expand in the context of the widening
historically wide distribution of
probable economic policy and market
outcomes in this fourth turning. So, uh
I've been saying this for years, if you
think you can trade your way through a
fourth turning polycrisis with all the,
you know, wacky things that are
happening from a growth, inflation,
monetary policy, fiscal policy,
liquidity, and positioning perspective,
then you have a tremendous amount of
hubris, and um your portfolio is
probably not going to be very kind uh to
you uh for having such a high degree of
hubris. So, transition to our
uh uh uh uh 42 macro dashboard. Boy,
this warmed my heart this morning, and
I'm sure it warmed yours as well, if
you're a human being and/or generally
supportive of of of, you know, creating
great outcomes for uh ordinary folks
like I like, you know, like I am. Uh
that's just why we started this firm. Uh
so, uh I'll just stop and read. It says,
"Your teaching is impacting my classroom
in a major way. Top three finish."
"Hello, DD. I'm an eighth grade business
teacher at a public school here in
Little Rock, Arkansas, and I'll be
honest, I thought I understood
investing. The reality is, I didn't. I
was making a little money simply because
the market was rising, but I wasn't
truly managing risk or maximizing
returns. I followed you for years, but
you know, pride had me around the
throat, SMH. Uh
One day in September, I heard you say,
'No matter if you miss the gold run or
not, it's never too late to get on the
right side of market risk.' I finally
decided to stop dating macro and finally
made a commitment. Uh I think he said,
'Stop dating 42 macro and finally made a
commitment.' Uh I have now been a
subscriber for about eight months, and
the difference has been significant. I
have been I have had more consistent
success, far less stress,
and more time to focus on what I love
most, teaching my students. Let me say
this again. I finally decided to stop
dating 42 macro and finally made the
commitment. I've now been a subscriber
for about eight months, and the
difference has been significant. I've
had more consistent success, far less
stress, and more time to focus on what I
love most, which is teaching my
students.
Uh in the classroom,
uh we actively follow the market and
participate in our state's stock market
game.
They They get $100,000 to trade real
time. We also watch and discuss your
YouTube videos almost daily. Your
principles principles have made such an
impact that one of my student teams
placed third out of 718
teams last semester, and earned the
superintendent citation award. I
guarantee you we would have
the team would have won on a
risk-adjusted basis. You can you can be
you can win with taking a maximum amount
of risk, but obviously you have to
respect the fact that markets are a
bimodal or two-sided with respect to
distribution. So, you can go buy the
highest beta asset and if it goes up,
you'll win any
count or any competition on any
short-term time horizon, but certainly
not on a risk-adjusted basis and
certainly not in a drawdown, which is
what we're currently experiencing. Who
knows when this ends? So, getting back
into this message. Says, "Your message
has truly stuck with them. On down
market days, students stop by my room
saying, 'Leave your emotions at the door
and wait for the text, lol.' They're
learning early that investors must stay
disciplined and follow the plan." "Today
after the Fed announcement, one even
left a note on my door that read, 'Leave
the emotions at home and wait for the
text, lol.'"
That kind of mindset shift is powerful,
not just for investing, but for life.
"My wife and I are both educators and
thanks in part to what we've learned
from you, we have far greater peace and
confidence in our financial approach.
What you're doing is making a real
difference even all the way down here in
a middle school classroom.
Please stay encouraged and keep going.
You're impacting more people than you
probably realize." So, um, I don't have
much to say after that. If you don't get
it after reading that, then you're never
going to get it and I'm totally fine
with that. You know, I've said this on
many occasions.
We can't help everybody. We're not going
to be able to save everybody from
themselves. Some people can't control
their emotions. Some people, you know,
they they you know, they they they don't
want to be improve their investing
habits. You know, they are comfortable
with the myriad of cognitive biases,
common behavioral heuristics that
prevent investors from achieving their
strategic objectives. This, you know,
based on our, you know, trending
attrition rate, that's roughly about 3.5
to 4% of of investors out there.
But for the 96 to 96.5% of investors out
there that want to improve their
investing habits, that want
significantly better results from a
portfolio perspective, that want
significantly lower stress, that want
